summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB, an in-process SQL analytical database engine he co-created at CWI in Amsterdam. Born from conversations with statisticians who needed powerful analytics without server setup complexity, DuckDB fills the gap between SQLite (transactional) and heavy distributed systems like Spark. He explains its vectorized query processing architecture, zero-copy integration with Python/R/Arrow, and the philosophy that most 'big data' workloads can run on a single machine. The project is governed by a non-profit foundation with MIT licensing, while DuckDB Labs provides commercial support.
key_points: DuckDB is an in-process OLAP engine that brings serious analytical capabilities wherever you need them—laptops, lambdas, browsers via WebAssembly—without server setup
A single DuckDB node matched the performance of a 32-node Spark cluster on taxi data benchmarks, exposing massive overuse of distributed infrastructure
The vectorized query processing design (vs JIT compilation) keeps DuckDB lightweight (~10MB binary) and debuggable while delivering high throughput
Zero-copy integration with pandas, R data frames, and Arrow enables seamless in-process data exchange, though it introduced novel memory ownership challenges
The project governance separates the non-profit DuckDB Foundation (holds IP, MIT license) from DuckDB Labs (commercial support), protecting the open source project
money_quotes: I really learned to appreciate the field of databases as a very no bullshit zone.
We literally talked to people, which is very rare for computer scientists, we like to talk to the computers, but maybe not so much to the people.
If you're going to be installed on everyone's laptop out there, you're going to have thousands of broken machines that you're running on.
Too often big data is just the same fairly small data copied a million times because people have no other choice than to replicate everything.
It's as if you drive a dump truck around as your daily driver. While Spark has its place, I also think it's complete overkill in like 99% of the use cases it's used for.
One node of DuckDB versus 32 nodes of Spark. All the compute is basically eaten up by the coordination that is designed to deal with a thousand node cluster.
There is a huge amount of headroom between where Pandas stops working and where you actually need something else. I would say that's at least two, three orders of magnitude.
We are kind of data communists in that sense that we treat all data sources equally.
key_insight: Most analytical workloads are massively over-provisioned with distributed infrastructure when a single-node in-process engine can deliver equivalent or superior performance with vastly less complexity and cost.
